title: Psalms 103
subtitle: Praise the LORD, My Soul

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — “As Far as the East Is from the West”

heb.gloss: to have compassion / to show mercy
heb.paragraph: "As a father has compassion (raḥam) on his children, so the LORD has compassion on those who fear him" (v.13). The greatest praise psalm. David commands his soul: praise! (v.1). Then the catalogue of benefits: forgives ALL sins, heals ALL diseases, redeems life from the pit, crowns with love and compassion, satisfies with good things, renews youth like the eagle’s (vv.2–5). God is slow to anger, abounding in love (v.8). He does not treat us as our sins deserve (v.10). "As far as the east is from the west, so far has he removed our transgressions from us" (v.12). The most beloved forgiveness verse in the Bible. East and west never meet — the distance is infinite.

notes.note: "Praise the LORD, my soul; all my inmost being, praise his holy name. Praise the LORD, my soul, and forget not all his benefits." Self-command to praise. "Forget not" — the antidote to ingratitude.

"Who forgives ALL your sins and heals ALL your diseases, who redeems your life from the pit, who crowns you with love and compassion, who satisfies your desires with good things so that your youth is renewed like the eagle’s." Five benefits: forgiveness, healing, redemption, crowning, satisfying. ALL is emphasized.
"The LORD is compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love. He does not treat us as our sins deserve." The Exodus 34:6 formula again. Grace exceeds justice.

"As high as the heavens are above the earth, so great is his love. As far as the east is from the west, so far has he removed our transgressions." Two unmeasurable distances: heaven-to-earth (vertical love) and east-to-west (horizontal removal). Infinite love, infinite forgiveness.

refs.note: Much forgiveness producing much love illustrates the psalm's theology of pardoned iniquities.
Prayer healing the sick and sins being forgiven echoes the psalm's connection.
Renewed strength and mounting up with wings like eagles parallels the psalm's eagle metaphor.

hist.context: Psalm 103 is a Davidic hymn of praise summarizing God's character. The opening self-address ('praise the LORD, my soul') summons the whole person to grateful worship. The benefits catalogued — forgiveness, healing, redemption, crowning with love — present salvation comprehensively rather than abstractly.

sections.header: Verses 13–22 — “From Everlasting to Everlasting the LORD’s Love Is with Those Who Fear Him”

heb.paragraph: "The life of mortals is like grass (ḥāṣîr); they flourish like a flower of the field; the wind blows over it and it is gone, and its place remembers it no more" (vv.15–16). Human transience: grass, flower, wind — gone. BUT: "from everlasting to everlasting the LORD’s love is with those who fear him" (v.17). Human life is brief; God’s love is eternal. The psalm closes by expanding the praise: angels, heavenly hosts, all his works, everywhere in his dominion — "Praise the LORD, my soul" (vv.20–22). The final word mirrors the first: the psalm is a circle of praise.

notes.note: "As a father has compassion on his children, so the LORD has compassion on those who fear him; for he knows how we are formed, he remembers that we are dust." The tenderness: God knows our composition. He remembers what we forget: we are dust. His expectations are calibrated to our capacity.

"The life of mortals is like grass — the wind blows over it and it is gone. But from everlasting to everlasting the LORD’s love is with those who fear him." The contrast: grass vs forever. Human life vs divine love. Transience vs eternity. We pass; love endures.

"The LORD has established his throne in heaven. Praise the LORD, you his angels. Praise the LORD, all his heavenly hosts. Praise the LORD, all his works everywhere in his dominion. Praise the LORD, my soul." The expanding circle: self → angels → heavenly hosts → all works everywhere. Then back to self: "praise the LORD, my soul." The universe praises; and so do I.

refs.note: The divine self-disclosure Moses received is quoted: compassionate, gracious, slow to anger.
God hurling sins into the depths of the sea parallels east-west removal.
Grass withering but God's word standing forever echoes the psalm's transience contrast.

hist.context: The east-west distance for sin removal (v.12) expresses immeasurable forgiveness. The father's compassion (v.13) introduces parental imagery rare in the Psalter. The psalm concludes by expanding praise from individual to cosmic: angels, heavenly hosts, all creation, and finally the self again — a worship spiral encompassing everything.

ppl.text: " The tenderness: God knows our composition…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVwho satisfies your desires with good things so that your youth is renewed like the eagle’s.ESVwho satisfies you with good so that your youth is renewed like the eagle\u2019s.NIVThe Lord is compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love.ESVThe LORD is merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love.

rec.text: ""Praise the LORD, my soul; all my inmost being, praise his holy name. Praise the LORD, my soul, and forget not all his benefits." Self-command to praise. "Forget not" — the antidote to ingratitude."

rows.label: Grass to Glory — 103:13–22
rows.text: "He knows we are dust." Grass dies; "love is from EVERLASTING TO EVERLASTING." Angels, hosts, all works — PRAISE.

chiasm.title: Psalm 103 — Praise for the Compassionate God

pairs.label: A
pairs.top: Praise the LORD, my soul; all my inmost being, praise his holy name. Praise the LORD, my soul, and forget not all his benefits (vv.1–2)
pairs.bottom: Praise the LORD, you his angels, you mighty ones who do his bidding. Praise the LORD, all his heavenly hosts. Praise the LORD, all his works. Praise the LORD, my soul (vv.20–22)

pairs.label: B
pairs.top: He forgives all your sins and heals all your diseases; he redeems your life from the pit and crowns you with love and compassion (vv.3–5)
pairs.bottom: For as high as the heavens are above the earth, so great is his love for those who fear him; as far as the east is from the west, so far has he removed our transgressions (vv.11–12)

pairs.label: C
pairs.top: He satisfies your desires with good things so that your youth is renewed like the eagle’s. The LORD works righteousness and justice for all the oppressed (vv.5–6)
pairs.bottom: As a father has compassion on his children, so the LORD has compassion on those who fear him; for he knows how we are formed, he remembers that we are dust (vv.13–14)

center.label: X
center.text: The LORD is compassionate and gracious, slow to anger, abounding in love. He will not always accuse, nor will he harbor his anger forever; he does not treat us as our sins deserve or repay us according to our iniquities (vv.8–10). The center is God’s character statement — echoing the self-revelation at Sinai (Exodus 34:6–7). Everything in the psalm radiates outward from this identity: God is compassionate. Period.

Then the catalogue of benefits: forgives ALL sins, heals ALL diseases, redeems life from the pit, crowns with love and compassion, satisfies with good things, renews youth like the eagle’s (vv.2–5). The most beloved forgiveness verse in the Bible. East and west never meet — the distance is infinite.

Human transience: grass, flower, wind — gone. BUT: "from everlasting to everlasting the LORD’s love is with those who fear him" (v.17). Human life is brief; God’s love is eternal. The final word mirrors the first: the psalm is a circle of praise.

themes.note: Mercy AND Worship BOTH peak at 10: the supreme praise psalm. East-to-west forgiveness. Father’s compassion. Everlasting-to-everlasting love. The expanding circle of universal praise.

prayer_prompt: Praise the LORD who forgives all your sins and heals all your diseases. Consider asking God about the benefits you've forgotten — what forgiveness and healing you take for granted.
